item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great scp overall, even with some errors. It serves a reminder that the scp foundation is full of strange objects, and just because they aren’t in -J doesn’t mean they can’t be a little funny. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject openly admitted that they were abused by their father as a child.

I mean this is probably just a small percentage of people who will notice this, but you've phrased this as if a child being abused has done something wrong by saying they "openly admit" to being abused. You generally "admit" to things that you've done that are wrong. And admit technically means to state "with reluctance" so you can't really "openly" admit.

Anyways "openly states" might be better, or "openly discusses their father's abuse of them as a child"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems a certain SCP decided to become a cowboy…

Not sure if I would call this original, but it's certainly an entertaining concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so stupid, it's great. Love it ! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First you state that the subject will always arrive within 24 hours, regardless of the distance, but in the very first test it takes 40 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Costume was kept by Researcher Fredrick.”

Hey uh…Freddy? Whatcha need that costume for

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love these whacky/absurd scips.

That and "delivered a kick with an estimated force of 5,000 newtons to the facility wall." Made me forcefully exhale. My only regret is not being able to upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… this is a nitpick, but the Peacemaker and Colt-45 are the same firearm, and the proper factory designation is either Single Action Army or Model 1873. Single Action Army or SAA is the more common term, but the military preferred the 1873 designation.

Just my $.02 on the issue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I want to nit-pick that hasn't already been mentioned is that it says that the outcome is random, but the testing logs have the -2 winning 3 times as often as -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Through the whole time I read this, Big Iron played in my head

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have several issues with this.

+ show block
– hide block

The anomaly is a straight-up compulsion. I figured we'd have an influx of extra-clichecon-yet-approved-of cliche articles now and in the following months, but given that this isn't an entry, I find it something to point out and disapprove of.

It's said in the description that

the surviving subject will drop their revolver and walk into the sunset. Five to ten minutes later, the remaining subject will resume normal behavior with no memory of the incident.

Yet the research staff gives several of the surviving participants amnestics after the observed 4873-GS events. Also "remaining subject" makes this needlessly ambiguous (even though one of the participants is surely dead); "surviving subject" would avoid that small issue. We are again told at the very end that the surviving participant has no recollection of the event, without amnestics. Also "into the sunset" is inappropriate tone, and might read better something like "Westward" or "to the West".

Site security were informed of the test and allowed D-16349 to proceed to the chamber.

I get that this is a compulsion effect, but this displays a remarkable lapse in judgment that could possibly have led to an in-facility shooting spree: giving a D-class a gun and letting them walk around the site freely. (That makes it not one but two of the cliches from the contest.)

Researcher Ramirez: Wait- Where did you get that? That's contraband!

It doesn't make sense that the research team would be familiar enough with the anomaly as to allow a D-class to walk through the site with a revolver, and yet here be surprised at the sudden appearance of Western paraphernalia.

D-16349: (Gets Closer)

Not sure why "closer" is capitalized.

Having said that, there are some golden moments in here. D-67839's GS event is very well done. Also these parts:

D-47592: So, how long are y'all gonna keep me in this here saloon?

Researcher Ramirez: (To Researcher) He's talking different, write that down.

D-16349 enters the room and strikes a pose.
D-16349: Gunslingin' Garry… Never thought I'd meet you here.
D-47592: (Stands up) Black Eyed Billy… To what do I owe the honor?

were really enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, I love the idea of just picking up a revolver one day and then have a wild west style duel.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-done, though I feel like the last test should be embellished to justify it being a separate addendum. Also wouldn't the Ethics Committee take issue with testing here? You're forcing random non-D-class civilians to face in a random deadly duel. +1 regardless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After dumping hours into Red Dead Redemption, this was a pleasant surprise to the mainlist. Take yer darn +1 cowpoke!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is a little unorthodox for your typical SCP, and it's just a tad bit too cliched/obvious (although that's probably what you were going for.)

Still though, this was quite the fun read overall and it was nice to read something that didn't get my critical mind going immediately. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to the Wild West! I got the idea for this during a history lecture, and thought it would work best as a goofier article. Thanks to Stallmantic and Laneous for critting.

Thanks for Reading!
